Investors are cashing-in massive gains:

Investors sold -$13.9 billion in US equities last week, driven entirely by single stocks.

This comes as single stocks saw -$14.2 billion in outflows, the largest weekly withdrawal in data going back to 2008.

At the same time, equity ETFs posted +$300 million in inflows, the 11th consecutive weekly intake.

Technology stocks led the sales, at -$10.8 billion, the highest weekly sale on record.

As a % of the S&P 500 technology sector's market cap, this was the largest sale since 2014.

Profit-taking is accelerating.

Kharg Island, which is one-third the size of Manhattan and located in the Persian Gulf, controls ~90% of Iranian crude oil exports.

That's roughly 1.5 to 2.0 million barrels of crude oil exports per day.

Trump specifically says this would be a "Venezuela" style takeover, where the US would take complete control of Iran's oil and gas infrastructure.

If this happens, it would be the biggest escalation of the Iran War yet.

We expect to receive Iran's response shortly.

tweets.text:
On this day in 2020, the Dow plunged 1,861 points, roughly 7%, as fears of a second wave of COVID-19 collided with a sobering press conference from Fed Chair Jerome Powell. Powell had warned the day before that the recovery would be long and uncertain, and that the Fed wasn't even thinking about raising rates for years.

The timing was brutal. The S&P 500 had just recovered nearly all of its pandemic losses, briefly turning positive for the year. Then COVID case counts started ticking up again across Sun Belt states, and Powell's tone made it clear the Fed saw a much longer road ahead than the market had been pricing in.

Three months later the market was at new all-time highs. The June 11 drop turned out to be one of the last great buying opportunities of the COVID era. Powell was right that recovery would take years for the real economy, but the stock market didn't wait around for it.

tweets.text:
This is a storm, not the end of a cycle.

I’m staying firm.

The hard asset cycle likely has much further to run.

For perspective:

Previous commodity supercycles unfolded during periods when the dollar was tied to gold, debt burdens were far lower, and globalization was accelerating.

Those were fundamentally different environments.

This cycle is being driven by monetary debasement, record leverage, fiscal excess, and the most significant wave of geopolitical fragmentation since the WWII.

Not the time to be shaken out of one of the most important macro theses of this decade.

https://t.co/yCHXf9QiO3
